artifacts.content:
Evan,

Kids are finally down and Olive has claimed my lap, so I’m debriefing to you instead of doom‑scrolling.

Work has felt especially heavy the last few months. On 12/27/23 I was in ICU room 412 for more than six hours straight in full PPE with that Code Blue on your Franklin High colleague. I coordinated the intubation with Ravi Patel, helped with round after round of open suctioning and bagging, and then had to call you from the staff break room to tell you he’d died. I still hear the vent alarms when I’m trying to fall asleep.

It keeps blending into everything else. Remember November 21, 2023, when I covered that night shift in the ED respiratory pod and spent about six hours in Room 12 with the 63‑year‑old COVID‑positive patient on remdesivir and q2 labs? I didn’t realize he was Karen Blake’s dad until she called my work cell at 11:30 p.m. sobbing. I keep telling myself I’m Infection Prevention, not everyone’s grief counselor, but that’s what it turns into.

And then there are the “quiet” things: Tuesday PCR clinics in Conference Room B all winter 2022–23, swabbing Carla from oncology and talking about her lupus and her parents in Gresham; late‑night debriefs with new grads like Emily outside ICU 409 about their third COVID death of the week.

Meanwhile we’re still trying to figure out whether to buy a house, juggle Maya’s climate‑club meetings, Jonah’s Lego obsession, and your Franklin outbreaks (I’m still not over your 12/9/23 positive after that lab cluster). Some nights it feels like our whole life is just different flavors of exposure tracking.

I don’t need you to fix anything. I just needed someone to know the whole picture.

L

metadata.subject: Trying to hold all of this

artifacts.content:
Dan,

Catching up between meetings on 3 West. You asked what “employee health” really looks like now, and honestly it’s a lot of one‑on‑one crisis management in a broom‑closet‑sized office.

On 8/19/24 I had Marcus Lee, one of our EVS techs, in my little 3 North office. He’d missed two shifts the prior week because his bipolar symptoms flared when he couldn’t get his lithium refill. The constant Code Blue calls on suspected COVID patients in the ICU are triggering panic attacks. I pointed him back to his OHSU psychiatrist and our EAP, and he begged me not to tell his charge nurse, Andrea Patel, anything about the diagnosis.

That same pattern keeps repeating. On 10/5/23 I spent 45 minutes with ICU charge nurse Kelly Thompson going over PTSD from the 2021 surge before we let her lead intubations and extubations in the negative‑pressure rooms again. On 1/27/22 I sat about 45 minutes with Emily Parsons in the tiny conference room outside ICU 409 while she sobbed about her third COVID death that week and told me she’s seeing a psychiatrist, Melissa Chang at Providence, after her dad died of COVID.

And it’s not just them. Between March and May 2024 I had around 15 post‑exposure fit‑testing/counseling sessions in Employee Health with people like Maria Alvarez and James O’Neill, going over vaccination status, pregnancy, immunosuppression, and whether their managers were pressuring them to skip N95s on short‑staffed nights.

Then I come home and we talk about your sprint deadlines and my kids’ spelling tests and it’s like I’m switching universes.

Anyway, this is why I sound fried when I call on my drive down SE Division.

Love,
Lauren

metadata.subject: What my days actually look like now

artifacts.content:
Personal work log – 2024-02-27

I need this in my own words so I don’t shove it in a corner of my brain and forget the details.

Today, while reviewing our COVID exposure logs, I realized I had to schedule post‑exposure testing for myself. I’d participated in manual ventilation during that ED Code Blue on a COVID‑positive patient earlier this month while I was 10 weeks pregnant. Only Hannah McKenzie at Women’s Health Associates knows. No one at Evergreen does, including Karen Liu.

I booked a PCR at the employee testing site on SE 82nd for 3/3/24 and blocked my Outlook as “medical appointment” so nothing pregnancy‑related pops up on shared calendars. It feels hypocritical to coach everyone else on transparency about risk and then hide my own, but I’m not ready for the whole hospital to know.

This is layered on top of everything else: December 9, 2023 when Evan tested positive after the Franklin High outbreak and I moved my work to the 4B respiratory cohort wing instead of the ED pod, documenting my own serial antigen testing every 48 hours and Maya and Jonah’s sniffles in my personal exposure log. January 14, 2024, 12‑hour shift in COVID‑capable ICU room 406 helping Dr. Lee intubate a 67‑year‑old from Mom’s Eugene book club while trying not to let on that I recognized her name. November 3, 2023, exposure assessment for Mark Liu after his N95 strap snapped during open suctioning in ICU 412.

I keep telling staff to protect their boundaries. I’m not sure I have any left.

metadata.title: Personal work log – pregnancy + exposure
metadata.location: Evergreen Valley Community Hospital, 3rd Floor Admin office

artifacts.content:
Mom,

Writing this instead of calling because I’ll cry if I hear your voice.

On 1/14/24 I worked a 12‑hour shift in the COVID‑capable ICU, room 406. Mid‑shift I assisted Dr. Lee with an emergent intubation on a 67‑year‑old woman. I didn’t put it together until I was doing oral care and charting that she’s in your Eugene book club. When you called that night asking if your friend was “the one on the ventilator in Portland,” I had to hide how shaken I was and fall back on the HIPAA script.

It brought me right back to 12/27/23 in ICU 412 with that 44‑year‑old unvaccinated COVID patient who turned out to be one of Evan’s Franklin colleagues. Over six hours in full PPE, coordinating intubation, open suctioning, bagging, then walking into the staff break room to call Evan and tell him his coworker had died.

In between those big traumas are the “smaller” ones: 10/3/23 in our ground‑floor Employee Health exam room with Jasmine, a 4B tech who was 10 weeks pregnant and terrified after a COVID exposure; 4/18/24 in my 3rd‑floor admin office with Mr. K. from Franklin High, going over his positive test and the lab he ran with Evan’s 10th‑graders; weekly winter 2022–23 PCR clinics in Conference Room B, swabbing Carla from oncology and talking about her lupus and her parents in Gresham.

Then I drive home past Mt. Tabor and switch to spelling lists, Jonah’s dinosaur facts, and whether your knees are behaving. It’s surreal.

I’m okay, just tired all the way down to my bones. Knowing you’re down in Eugene cheering on Maya’s marine‑biology rants helps more than you know.

Love,
Lauren

Event: COVID PCR Clinic – high‑risk staff
Time: Tuesdays, 6:30–9:00 a.m.
Location: Conference Room B, Evergreen Valley Community Hospital
Attendees: rotating high‑risk staff (incl. oncology, ICU, ED)

Notes:

Winter 2022–2023 surge routine. Set up swabbing station by 6:15 a.m. so people can get through before 7:00 shifts. Prioritize:
- Oncology staff (esp. Carla M. – lupus on chronic low‑dose prednisone, worried about elderly parents in Gresham).
- ICU COVID‑capable team (charge nurses, RTs like Mark Liu, techs such as Maria Alvarez).
- ED respiratory pod crew.

Budget 20–30 minutes most weeks for Carla alone; she tends to arrive early and needs space to talk through fears about bringing COVID home. Remember to check that we’ve stocked enough NIOSH‑approved N95s for anyone heading straight to negative‑pressure rooms afterward.

Also block time after clinic for:
- Quick charting in Employee Health.
- Follow‑up scheduling for staff with household exposures (e.g., my own December 9, 2023 household exposure when Evan tested positive after the Franklin High outbreak; used this same slot on 12/12 and 12/14 for my serial antigen testing).
- Brief huddles with ICU manager Elena Rodriguez about staffing gaps and who might need modified duty (e.g., Julia Bennett’s post‑COVID POTS plan in April 2024 – PPE observer + chart audits only for six weeks in the ED respiratory pod).

Personal reminder: grab coffee and a real breakfast after clinic before walking into back‑to‑back counseling sessions; these mornings are emotionally heavier than they look on the schedule.

metadata.title: COVID PCR Clinic – high‑risk staff (winter 22–23)
metadata.location: Conference Room B, Evergreen Valley Community Hospital

artifacts.content:
Evan,

Quick download before I forget the details and blur them all together.

Today (5/6/24) I met Jason Nguyen, one of our RTs, in the small ICU conference room. He asked to be taken off all COVID AGPs for at least three months because his 4‑year‑old at Buckman Preschool is getting chemo for ALL at Doernbecher. He even brought a letter from the oncologist recommending he avoid open suctioning, BiPAP setups, and manual bagging on COVID‑positive patients. I’ll have to juggle the RT schedule without broadcasting his son’s diagnosis.

It reminded me of 11/3/24, when I pulled ICU charge nurse Kelly Ramirez aside after huddle. She quietly told me she was 14 weeks pregnant and panicking about Code Blue assignments in the COVID‑capable pod. We agreed I’d try to keep her off the negative‑pressure rooms for a couple of months while she decides when to tell HR.

And back in February I had to do the same calculus for myself after that ED Code Blue where I ended up bagging a COVID‑positive patient while 10 weeks pregnant. I scheduled my own PCR at the employee testing site on SE 82nd for 3/3 and hid it in my calendar as a generic “medical appointment” so Karen wouldn’t see anything.

All of this on top of the usual: twice‑weekly 3–5 p.m. observation blocks in the ED triage bay coaching people like Kelly Donnelly on N95 use while she’s still short of breath from her own COVID + asthma combo; return‑to‑work calls like the 25‑minute one on 5/29/24 with Dr. Anil Shah about his post‑COVID brain fog after a 10‑day isolation.

Can we please still do no‑screens Thursday this week? I need Settlers of Catan and Maya’s climate rants more than Netflix.

L

metadata.subject: People I’m trying to protect (including me)

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – counseling & boundaries

Dates to remember:

9/5/24 – Spent ~30 minutes in my office with ED RN Hannah Lewis. She’s 18 weeks pregnant, living in a small apartment off SE 82nd with her unvaccinated 2‑year‑old, and keeps getting assigned to the ED respiratory pod. She was in tears about bringing COVID home. I offered to talk with staffing about rotating her out more without outing her pregnancy before she’s ready.

5/2/24 – 45‑minute return‑to‑work visit with ED tech Maria Santos in my little office off the ED respiratory pod. We went through her household exposures: husband on chemotherapy (immunosuppressed), teenage son unvaccinated. Cleared her for negative‑pressure rooms but only after a long conversation about PPE and her comfort level.

10/17/24 – Triage bay observation, 3–5 p.m. Spent time within 3–4 feet of multiple coughing patients while coaching triage RN Kelly Donnelly on mask fit. Had to remind her to keep her N95 on; she’s still short of breath from a recent COVID infection and asthma flare.

1/9/24 – In ED respiratory pod Room R‑13 for ~25 minutes, shoulder‑to‑shoulder with Mark Ellison and RT Dana Wu, troubleshooting a leaking BiPAP mask on a severely hypoxic, coughing COVID‑positive patient who kept ripping the mask off in agitation.

Thread through all of this: I’m supposed to be the boundary‑keeper, but my job has become absorbing everyone’s fear – pregnant staff, people with sick kids, folks with messy home situations – while I’m also quietly tracking my own exposures, Evan’s Franklin outbreaks, Mom’s aging joints, and the kids’ school colds.

Need to keep running at Mt. Tabor on weekends. It’s the only place this doesn’t follow me.
